nodes.x: I am come into my garden, my sister, my spouse: I have gathered my myrrh with my spice; I have eaten my honeycomb with my honey; I have drunk my wine with my milk: eat, O friends; drink, yea, drink…
¶ My beloved is mine, and I am his: he feedeth among the lilies.
Come, eat of my bread, and drink of the wine which I have mingled.
I am my beloved’s, and my beloved is mine: he feedeth among the lilies.
¶ Awake, O north wind; and come, thou south; blow upon my garden, that the spices thereof may flow out. Let my beloved come into his garden, and eat his pleasant fruits.
My beloved is gone down into his garden, to the beds of spices, to feed in the gardens, and to gather lilies.

¶ I sleep, but my heart waketh: it is the voice of my beloved that knocketh, saying, Open to me, my sister, my love, my dove, my undefiled: for my head is filled with dew, and my locks with the drops…
And as ye shall lift up your voices by the Comforter, ye shall speak and prophesy as seemeth me good;
Truly, in many places the people seem as though a spirit of deep sleep was upon them. They have eyes but see not, ear 3 but hear not, hearts but do not understand.
Behold, I shew you a mystery; We shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed,
And the roof of thy mouth like the best wine for my beloved, that goeth down sweetly, causing the lips of those that are asleep to speak.
Yet heard I the voice of his words: and when I heard the voice of his words, then was I in a deep sleep on my face, and my face toward the ground.
I have put off my coat; how shall I put it on? I have washed my feet; how shall I defile them?
But be shod with sandals; and not put on two coats.
If I then, your Lord and Master, have washed your feet; ye also ought to wash one another’s feet.
For I said, Hear me, lest otherwise they should rejoice over me: when my foot slippeth, they magnify themselves against me.

On the 23rd of January, we again assembled in conference; when, after
much speaking, singing, praying, and praising God, all in tongues, we
proceeded to the washing of feet (according to the practice recorded
in the 13th chapter of John's Gospel), as commanded of the Lord. Each
Elder washed his own feet first, after which I girded myself with a
towel and washed the feet of all of them, wiping…
By the great force of my disease is my garment changed: it bindeth me about as the collar of my coat.
My beloved put in his hand by the hole of the door, and my bowels were moved for him.
I rose up to open to my beloved; and my hands dropped with myrrh, and my fingers with sweet smelling myrrh, upon the handles of the lock.
18 I know that my hand found not the wherewithal to give to him that needed, and I walked with him weeping for seven furlongs, and my bowels yearned towards him in compassion.
¶ And he brought me to the door of the court; and when I looked, behold a hole in the wall.
I rose up to open to my beloved; and my hands dropped with myrrh, and my fingers with sweet smelling myrrh, upon the handles of the lock.
My beloved put in his hand by the hole of the door, and my bowels were moved for him.
¶ And, behold, an hand touched me, which set me upon my knees and upon the palms of my hands.
And my heart hath been secretly enticed, or my mouth hath kissed my hand:
Now the hand of the Lord was upon me in the evening, afore he that was escaped came; and had opened my mouth, until he came to me in the morning; and my mouth was opened, and I was no more dumb.

But, happily for me, there was a woman spinning just outside the door
of the little house on the hill, who, with the instinctive watchfulness
which mothers have for all children, had kept her eye upon us all the
time, and when I was tilted into the spring, she ran quickly down,
seized me by the heels, drew me out, and carried me, all white and
cold as death, to my mother. By the time she reached…
I opened to my beloved; but my beloved had withdrawn himself, and was gone: my soul failed when he spake: I sought him, but I could not find him; I called him, but he gave me no answer.
By night on my bed I sought him whom my soul loveth: I sought him, but I found him not.
I looked on my right hand, and beheld, but there was no man that would know me: refuge failed me; no man cared for my soul.
Yet he passed away, and, lo, he was not: yea, I sought him, but he could not be found.
Now, after the Lord had withdrawn from speaking to me, and withdrawn his face from me, I said in my heart: Thy servant has sought thee earnestly; now I have found thee;

Thus ended an intimacy with one who had j rendered himself doubly dear to me by an intimate acquaintance. I have met but few men in my life with whom I could exchange feelings, reciprocate joys and sorrows, as we pass down the step of life, more sympathetically
The watchmen that went about the city found me, they smote me, they wounded me; the keepers of the walls took away my veil from me.
The watchmen that go about the city found me: to whom I said, Saw ye him whom my soul loveth?
And now, I desire to know the cause whereby ye were so bold as to come near the walls of the city, when I, myself, was with my guards without the gate?
¶ Now when they were going, behold, some of the watch came into the city, and shewed unto the chief priests all the things that were done.
And stay ye not, but pursue after your enemies, and smite the hindmost of them; suffer them not to enter into their cities: for the Lord your God hath delivered them into your hand.
I charge you, O daughters of Jerusalem, if ye find my beloved, that ye tell him, that I am sick of love.
I charge you, O daughters of Jerusalem, that ye stir not up, nor awake my love, until he please.
I charge you, O ye daughters of Jerusalem, by the roes, and by the hinds of the field, that ye stir not up, nor awake my love, till he please.
Pray for the peace of Jerusalem: they shall prosper that love thee.

And I will not suffer, saith the Lord of Hosts, that the cries of the fair daughters of this people, which I have led out of the land of Jerusalem, shall come up unto me against the men of my people, saith the Lord of Hosts.
¶ What is thy beloved more than another beloved, O thou fairest among women? what is thy beloved more than another beloved, that thou dost so charge us?
Whither is thy beloved gone, O thou fairest among women? whither is thy beloved turned aside? that we may seek him with thee.
Since thou wast precious in my sight, thou hast been honourable, and I have loved thee: therefore will I give men for thee, and people for thy life.
Beloved, thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest to the brethren, and to strangers;
Shew thy marvellous lovingkindness, O thou that savest by thy right hand them which put their trust in thee from those that rise up against them.
Honour thy father and thy mother: and, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself.
My beloved is white and ruddy, the chiefest among ten thousand.

"Now, from out these rude habitations, through many a rent and flapping door, the children of the desert are pouring — ^a curious lot of black-headed, squatty-figured little women and children. Sublime picture of blissful indiflFerence ! You do not know that this is your last day of undisputed reign — if your sleepy existence can be called a reign. Before the siin shall have reached the zenith,…

10. And with them the Head of Days,
    His head white and pure as wool,
    And His raiment indescribable.
His head is as the most fine gold, his locks are bushy, and black as a raven.
This image’s head was of fine gold, his breast and his arms of silver, his belly and his thighs of brass,
His hands are as gold rings set with the beryl: his belly is as bright ivory overlaid with sapphires.

He was tall and broad-shouldered, and limped with the gout; his face was
copper-colored, and his eyes were dark set, with bloated lids, and
eyebrows bushy as his beard; his head was close shaven behind in Turkish
fashion, and he wore a cap night and day, and over his brow hung a
braided lock of hair. The hide of his bull-neck rose above his stiff
collar; his fat chin covered his neckerchief, tied…
And the flowers, and the lamps, and the tongs, made he of gold, and that perfect gold;
And every raven after his kind,
His eyes are as the eyes of doves by the rivers of waters, washed with milk, and fitly set.
By his neesings a light doth shine, and his eyes are like the eyelids of the morning.
He is swift as the waters; their portion is cursed in the earth: he beholdeth not the way of the vineyards.
He cutteth out rivers among the rocks; and his eye seeth every precious thing.
His eyes shall be red with wine, and his teeth white with milk.
For his eyes are upon the ways of man, and he seeth all his goings.
His cheeks are as a bed of spices, as sweet flowers: his lips like lilies, dropping sweet smelling myrrh.
His mouth is most sweet: yea, he is altogether lovely. This is my beloved, and this is my friend, O daughters of Jerusalem.
Though wickedness be sweet in his mouth, though he hide it under his tongue;

He was, as has been said, yellow, and his curling, silky hair was soft
as flax, with "silver threads among the gold" about the neck and breast.
His liquid brown eyes were "just too sweet," as Edith declared, while
his inquisitive little nose, although not as black as it should be for
beauty, was nevertheless eloquent with expression; and his tail, mere
stub of a one as it was, did duty for a…
Lying lips are abomination to the Lord: but they that deal truly are his delight.
His hands are as gold rings set with the beryl: his belly is as bright ivory overlaid with sapphires.
His head is as the most fine gold, his locks are bushy, and black as a raven.
And he cast for it four rings of gold, to be set by the four corners of it; even two rings upon the one side of it, and two rings upon the other side of it.

His body also was like the beryl, and his face as the appearance of lightning, and his eyes as lamps of fire, and his arms and his feet like in colour to polished brass, and the voice of his words like the voice of a multitude.
His legs are as pillars of marble, set upon sockets of fine gold: his countenance is as Lebanon, excellent as the cedars.
His legs of iron, his feet part of iron and part of clay.
His branches shall spread, and his beauty shall be as the olive tree, and his smell as Lebanon.
And the king made silver to be in Jerusalem as stones, and cedars made he to be as the sycomore trees that are in the vale, for abundance.
His mouth is most sweet: yea, he is altogether lovely. This is my beloved, and this is my friend, O daughters of Jerusalem.
He that loveth pureness of heart, for the grace of his lips the king shall be his friend.
His cheeks are as a bed of spices, as sweet flowers: his lips like lilies, dropping sweet smelling myrrh.
Righteous lips are the delight of kings; and they love him that speaketh right.
My meditation of him shall be sweet: I will be glad in the Lord.
How sweet are thy words unto my taste! yea, sweeter than honey to my mouth!
